title: When causal inference meets deep learning

data_scope.article_type: News and Views / Brief Communication (no original data collected)
data_scope.benchmark_graph_sizes: 10-100+ nodes (synthetic DAGs, varies by primary paper)
data_scope.noise_models: linear Gaussian, additive non-Gaussian, nonlinear post-nonlinear
data_scope.real_data: none in this commentary; primary papers may use real benchmarks

core_conclusion: Bayesian networks can capture causal relations, but learning such a network from data is NP-hard. Recent work — particularly NOTEARS (Zheng et al., 2018) and its deep extensions — reformulates DAG structure learning as a continuous optimization problem, enabling scalable gradient-based solvers. Pairing this with deep learning bridges interpretable causal graphs with nonlinear representational power.
why_it_matters: Breaks the NP-hard combinatorial barrier for causal DAG learning via a smooth differentiable acyclicity constraint; unites causal interpretability with deep learning expressiveness; opens tractable counterfactual reasoning without randomized experiments; synthesizes a rapid burst of concurrent 2018-2020 papers for field navigation.
why_nature: Timely synthesis at a field inflection point bridging statistics, ML, and causal reasoning; cross-disciplinary relevance across healthcare, social science, and scientific discovery; highlights a fundamental limitation (NP-hardness) and its algorithmic resolution; maps open frontiers for the research community.
added_value_to_my_research: Scalable DAG learner on spatiotemporal graphs -> recovers directed causal flows between spatial units for GeoAI
DAG-constrained GNN as structural prior -> inherently interpretable spatial prediction replacing SHAP post-hoc
Do-calculus on recovered DAG -> counterfactual facility-intervention queries for healthcare accessibility policy
Causal graph as decision artifact -> replaces black-box XAI explanations in high-stakes urban planning
Active learning + DAG validation -> prioritizes which spatial policy pilots most efficiently validate causal structure

figures.caption: Causal inference with deep learning — (a) DAG-based causal modeling; (b) linear SEM formulation; (c) NOTEARS continuous acyclicity constraint; (d) VAE-based nonlinear SEM (DAG-GNN); (e) GraN-DAG weight-based approach; (f) nonparametric SEM extension
